Best use case: Greenfield or brownfield renewable, storage and grid assets with ring-fenced cash flows and bankable contracts.
Key KPIs or covenants: Construction completion tests, DSCR, reserve accounts, EPC and O&M covenants, eligible green asset tests
Advantages: Strong lender control, long tenor, suited to SPVs and syndication for large assets
Risks / limitations: Execution risk during construction, documentation intensity, less efficient for small fragmented assets

Best use case: Use-of-proceeds financing for eligible capex, retrofit programmes or portfolio build-out at the corporate level.
Typical borrower / issuer: Corporates, developers, real-estate owners, financial institutions
Key KPIs or covenants: Eligible use-of-proceeds schedule, reporting undertakings, leverage and cash-sweep terms where relevant
Advantages: Faster than bond issuance, flexible drawdown, works for repeat capex programmes
Risks / limitations: Needs clear asset eligibility and reporting discipline; tenor is shorter than project finance

Best use case: Portfolio refinancing, operating asset take-outs and repeat funding for issuers with credible reporting and market access.
Typical borrower / issuer: Investment-grade corporates, utilities, infrastructure funds, financial institutions
Key KPIs or covenants: Use-of-proceeds ring-fencing, allocation reporting, impact reporting, negative pledge or standard bond covenants
Advantages: Scalable refinancing tool, opens institutional capital pools, lowers dependence on bank balance sheets
Risks / limitations: Requires market access, disclosure readiness and sufficient issuance size; less efficient during construction

Best use case: Refinancing diversified pools of operating assets such as rooftop solar, EV receivables or efficiency portfolios.
Typical borrower / issuer: Financial institutions, NBFCs, aggregators, infrastructure funds
Key KPIs or covenants: Pool eligibility criteria, delinquency triggers, over-collateralisation, servicing and reporting covenants
Advantages: Recycles balance-sheet capacity and matches fragmented operating assets to institutional demand
Risks / limitations: Requires asset history, data quality and standardisation; execution is inefficient before portfolios season

Best use case: Bridge and warehouse funding for fragmented clean-asset portfolios before securitisation, bond issuance or take-out.
Typical borrower / issuer: NBFCs, aggregators, distributed solar platforms, EV and storage originators
Key KPIs or covenants: Borrowing-base tests, concentration limits, asset-eligibility criteria, amortisation triggers
Advantages: Ideal for distributed assets that are too small for direct bond markets and need aggregation first
Risks / limitations: Relies on servicing quality, collateral data and reliable take-out strategy

Best use case: Replacement of construction debt once operating history exists, often ahead of public-market issuance.
Key KPIs or covenants: Operating history tests, minimum DSCR, refinancing timeline, distribution lock-up triggers
Advantages: Creates a practical bridge from bank construction debt to institutional capital
Risks / limitations: Needs operating history and clear visibility on permanent capital take-out

Best use case: Credit enhancement, first-loss support or risk-sharing for newer technologies, SME borrowers or transition portfolios.
Typical borrower / issuer: DFIs, guarantors, commercial banks, platform lenders
Key KPIs or covenants: Coverage ratio, portfolio eligibility, claim mechanics, reporting and utilization tests
Advantages: Improves bankability where tenor, technology or borrower quality would otherwise be constrained
Risks / limitations: Availability depends on guarantor appetite; claim structures can be complex

Best use case: General corporate purpose or capex financing where pricing is linked to borrower-level sustainability KPIs.
Typical borrower / issuer: Corporates, EPCs, mobility operators, financial institutions
Key KPIs or covenants: SPTs tied to emissions, renewable share, energy intensity, recycling, safety or supply-chain metrics; margin ratchet
Advantages: Works even when funds are not ring-fenced, good transition tool for diversified borrowers, can scale across relationship banks
Risks / limitations: KPI calibration can be weak if targets are not ambitious; reputational risk if SPTs are not credible

Best use case: KPI-linked revolving liquidity for diversified corporates and operating businesses with variable funding needs.
Key KPIs or covenants: Sustainability margin ratchet, liquidity covenant package, annual KPI assurance
Advantages: Well suited to working-capital variability and treasury-led adoption of sustainability-linked financing
Risks / limitations: Shorter tenor and limited direct capex linkage reduce visibility for pure asset financing

Best use case: Capital markets financing for larger issuers that can commit to public transition KPIs and step-up structures.
Typical borrower / issuer: Large corporates and utilities with public debt access
Key KPIs or covenants: Public SPTs, coupon step-up or alternative economics, annual assurance and external review
Advantages: Balance-sheet flexibility with public-market sizing and visible transition signaling
Risks / limitations: Public scrutiny is high, weak targets are penalized, and sub-investment-grade execution can be volatile

Best use case: Financing for decarbonisation pathways in hard-to-abate sectors where assets or plans may not be fully green today.
Typical borrower / issuer: Steel, cement, chemicals, transport, diversified industrial corporates
Key KPIs or covenants: Transition plan milestones, capex deployment tests, emissions-intensity pathways, leverage and liquidity covenants
Advantages: Useful where activities are transition-enabling but not fully taxonomy-green; preserves bank dialogue on strategy
Risks / limitations: Framework scrutiny is high; eligibility boundaries can be contentious and transaction preparation can be heavy

Best use case: Capital markets instrument for transition capex or refinancing under a documented transition framework.
Typical borrower / issuer: Large corporates and utilities in hard-to-abate sectors
Key KPIs or covenants: Transition framework, eligible project list or transition-plan disclosures, reporting and assurance
Advantages: Diversifies funding for large transition issuers and can refinance multiple projects at once
Risks / limitations: Investor acceptance depends on framework credibility and issuer disclosure quality

Best use case: Hybrid transition instrument combining public KPI commitments with broader transition funding flexibility.
Typical borrower / issuer: Large corporates and utilities with transition frameworks
Key KPIs or covenants: Transition KPIs, coupon step-up, annual progress reporting, external review
Advantages: Useful when issuers need both transition signaling and balance-sheet flexibility
Risks / limitations: Execution can be difficult if investors see KPIs as weak or transition plan as incomplete

Best use case: Private debt or note placement for issuers that want transition financing without full benchmark-bond execution.
Typical borrower / issuer: Large mid-cap corporates, infrastructure platforms, family-owned industrial groups
Key KPIs or covenants: Investor reporting package, transition disclosures, leverage and incurrence covenant suite
Advantages: Broadens investor access for issuers that are not frequent public benchmark borrowers
Risks / limitations: Pricing and investor depth can be less predictable than syndicated or benchmark bonds

Product: ESG-linked working capital / sustainable trade finance
Best use case: Supply-chain finance, letters of credit, receivables and working-capital lines tied to sustainability performance or eligible trade flows.
Typical borrower / issuer: EPCs, manufacturers, exporters, mobility operators, financial institutions
Key KPIs or covenants: Working-capital borrowing base, sustainability margin ratchet, supplier onboarding criteria, eligible trade-flow tests
Advantages: Fits procurement and execution cycles, can reach supply chains, useful bridge before term debt or bonds
Risks / limitations: Short tenor and operational complexity; not a substitute for long-dated capex finance

Best use case: Higher-risk, first-loss or concessional structures for new technologies, underserved segments or early-market platforms.
Typical borrower / issuer: Developers, financial institutions, platform aggregators, public-private partnerships
Key KPIs or covenants: Concessional tranche terms, eligibility criteria, policy-linked milestones, reporting to catalytic capital providers
Advantages: Crowds in commercial lenders, absorbs early-stage risk, useful for storage, distributed assets and nascent technologies
Risks / limitations: Structuring is slower, concessional capital is scarce, and governance can be complex

Best use case: Revenue support or incentive structures linked to verified emissions reductions, carbon credits or outcomes-based programmes.
Typical borrower / issuer: Project developers, industrial corporates, building portfolios, platform operators
Key KPIs or covenants: Monitoring, reporting and verification, credit issuance rules, delivery obligations, buffer or reserve mechanics
Advantages: Can improve project economics for efficiency, nature and early-transition projects that need incremental revenue
Risks / limitations: Price volatility, policy uncertainty and verification complexity limit bankability as a stand-alone funding source

Best use case: Green, transition or sustainability-linked bond and private-placement advisory for issuers preparing institutional capital raises.
Typical borrower / issuer: Issuers with capital-markets ambitions, utilities, infrastructure funds, large corporates
Key KPIs or covenants: Framework readiness, investor positioning, disclosure package, second-party opinion or review process
Advantages: Important scaling tool once issuers are ready to move from bank-led financing to institutional capital
Risks / limitations: Not a funding instrument on its own; depends on successful market execution and issuer readiness
